frameworks.name: Veil of Ignorance (Rawlsian)
frameworks.one_line: Choice procedure that hides one's relative position to elicit fair principles

frameworks.name: AI alignment / governance
frameworks.one_line: Applies a political-philosophy device to selecting principles for AI assistants

frameworks.name: risk-attitude and political-preference accounts
frameworks.one_line: Tested as alternative explanations for prioritarian choice and rejected

frameworks.name: fairness-based moral reasoning
frameworks.one_line: Spontaneous fairness justifications proposed as mediator of the VoI effect

core_question: Does reasoning behind the Veil of Ignorance lead people to choose AI-governance principles that prioritize the worst-off?
key_assumptions: Choices in incentive-compatible vignettes proxy real principle endorsement
Hiding relative position approximates Rawls' veil
Self-reported justifications index the basis of choice
manipulations: Veil of Ignorance vs Control (knowledge of own position)
Choice of governing principle for an AI assistant
key_findings: VoI participants more often chose a principle prioritizing the worst-off
Risk attitudes and political preferences did not adequately explain choices
VoI participants spontaneously cited fairness more often than Controls
VoI increased likelihood of re-endorsing the initial choice in a later round
interpretations: The Veil of Ignorance shifts AI-governance preferences toward prioritarian fairness
VoI elicits more robust, fairness-driven preferences rather than mere risk aversion
